name: Piedrasluengas and Collado de Hoz Loop
name_original: Piedrasluengas, Collado de Hoz, Puentenansa – ruta circular
description: This difficult 65.2-mile (105.0 km) road cycling route through the Cantabrian Mountains offers significant climbs and diverse scenery.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Tudanca are characterized by the mountainous terrain of Cantabria, offering diverse routes with significant elevation changes. The region is nestled within the Nansa Valley, featuring lush green landscapes and the Nansa River. Roads are generally well-maintained and experience low traffic levels, providing a suitable environment for road cyclists. Tudanca's location provides access to areas within or near natural parks like Saja-Besaya, ensuring varied natural features along the routes. Best road cycling routes around Tudanca The most popular road cycling route is Piedrasluengas and Collado de Hoz Loop, a 65.2 miles (105.0 km) trail that takes 5 hours 55 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant climbs and mountain passes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Port of Palombera and Alto de San Cipriano from Ruente – circular through the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, a difficult 69.2 miles (111.3 km) path. This route traverses the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, offering varied scenery and challenging ascents. Local road cyclists also love the Stone Fountain loop from Ruente, a 20.4 miles (32.9 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 1 hour 22 minutes. Road cycling around Tudanca is defined by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and natural parks, with well-maintained roads and challenging climbs. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easier valley rides to demanding mountain ascents. The routes in Tudanca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars. More than 600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Tudanca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Tudanca?
A: There are over 10 dedicated road cycling routes around Tudanca, catering to various skill levels. You'll find options ranging from easy valley rides to challenging mountain ascents.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in the Tudanca region?
A: The Tudanca region, nestled in Cantabria's Nansa Valley, offers diverse mountainous terrain. You can expect significant elevation changes, from serene valley roads alongside rivers to demanding climbs up to peaks like Peña Labra and Tres Mares. Roads are generally well-maintained and experience low traffic, especially away from major urban centers.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or a more relaxed ride?
A: Yes, there are a couple of easy routes. For a more relaxed experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40350952" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Stone Fountain loop from Ruente&lt;/a&gt;, which covers about 33 km with moderate elevation gain, perfect for enjoying the scenery without extreme effort.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Experienced riders seeking a challenge will find several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004542538/piedrasluengas-and-collado-de-hoz-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedrasluengas and Collado de Hoz Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 105 km route featuring significant climbs and mountain passes. Another excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004913144/port-of-palombera-and-alto-de-san-cipriano-from-ruente-circular-through-the-saja-besaya-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Palombera and Alto de San Cipriano from Ruente&lt;/a&gt;, a 111 km circular route through the Saja-Besaya Natural Park with varied scenery and challenging ascents.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can enjoy views towards the Picos de Europa from elevated points, or cycle through the protected forests of the Saja-Besaya Natural Park. Notable viewpoints include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839687" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tres Mares Peak&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839708" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Labra&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic vistas of the Cantabrian interior. The Nansa River also provides serene stretches along its valley.

Q: Are there any waterfalls accessible or visible from the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region features beautiful waterfalls. You might encounter the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/994831" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lamiña Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt;, located near Barcenillas within the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5469498" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo del Infierno Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, offering a refreshing stop amidst your ride.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes around Tudanca?
A: The road cycling routes around Tudanca are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the stunning natural landscapes, the challenge of the climbs, and the generally quiet, well-maintained roads that make for a peaceful and enjoyable ride.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes starting near Tudanca?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40347857" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Stone Fountain – Collada de Carmona loop from Ruente&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 40 km ride that offers varied terrain and scenic views. Several other routes, including the more challenging ones, are also circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in the Tudanca area?
A: The best time for road cycling in the Tudanca area, and Cantabria generally, is during the spring and autumn months. The weather is typically milder, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can also be pleasant, especially in the mountains, but be prepared for warmer temperatures. Winters can be cold and wet, with snow at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any unique landmarks or points of interest to explore along the routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you might encounter interesting landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3671607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuentona de Ruente Bridge&lt;/a&gt; is a notable point of interest. Additionally, the region is dotted with charming villages and traditional architecture, offering glimpses into local culture as you cycle through.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain for road cycling routes in this region?
A: The Tudanca region is characterized by its mountainous terrain, so elevation gain can vary significantly. Easy routes might have a few hundred meters of ascent, while difficult routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004542538/piedrasluengas-and-collado-de-hoz-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedrasluengas and Collado de Hoz Loop&lt;/a&gt; can feature over 2,300 meters of climbing, providing ample challenge for those seeking significant elevation.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through natural parks?
A: Absolutely. Tudanca's location provides excellent access to areas within or near natural parks. For example, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004913144/port-of-palombera-and-alto-de-san-cipriano-from-ruente-circular-through-the-saja-besaya-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Palombera and Alto de San Cipriano from Ruente&lt;/a&gt; route traverses the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, offering varied scenery and unspoiled natural beauty.
tips: This is the toughest and most beautiful part at the same time, with sections between 8% and 9% until the ascent to the Cohilla reservoir.

The section between Linares and La Hermida will be a great surprise. It is a winding mountain road that will leave you with tired legs and a feeling of joy at being able to enjoy such unparalleled views.

On the way down, I suggest you visit the Romanesque church of Santa Juliana, right at the entrance to the town of Lafuente.

The church of Santa Juliana is located in the town of Lafuente. This 12-13th century building is a sample of Romanesque art in the Nansa area.

Near it, you will find a hostel for pilgrims.
It has a length of almost 6 km, an altitude of 554 meters and an average slope of 5.62%.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 105 kilometers long and is estimated to take around 5 hours and 55 minutes to complete for a rider with very good fitness. This duration can vary based on individual pace and stops.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as "difficult" and requires very good fitness due to its significant length (105 km) and substantial elevation gain (over 2,350 meters). It is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features mostly well-paved surfaces, typical for race biking. However, it's important to note that some segments may be unpaved and difficult to ride, potentially requiring you to push your bike. The final 3 kilometers of the Piedrasluengas climb, especially after crossing into Castilla y León, can be bumpy and cracked.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable features. The route includes the challenging Ascent to the Cohilla Reservoir, known for its interesting scenery and steepest sections. You'll also pass the Linares to La Hermida Mountain Road and the Collado de Ozalba (Collado de Hoz), which has been featured in the Vuelta a España. From the Piedrasluengas viewpoint, you can enjoy impressive vistas of the Picos de Europa, Peña Labra, and the Liébana Valley.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The route is generally accessible year-round. However, be aware that temporary closures due to snow are possible during winter months in the higher elevations of the Cantabrian Mountains.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting and ending around Puentenansa. While specific parking areas aren't detailed, Puentenansa would be a logical starting point with potential parking options in the village.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for road cycling, which typically means dogs are not suitable companions, especially given the challenging nature and potential for traffic. It's best to leave pets at home for this particular ride.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no special permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route through the Cantabrian Mountains.

faqs.question: Are there any particularly challenging sections or specific considerations for this route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Piedrasluengas climb from Puentenansa is a long slog at 37.4 km. While generally good, the final 3 kilometers, especially after crossing into Castilla y León, can be notably worse with bumpy and cracked surfaces. Be prepared for potentially unpaved segments where you might need to push your bike. The Collado de Hoz climb, however, offers a good road surface and less traffic.

faqs.question: Are there any places to stop for refreshments or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several villages, including Puentenansa, which would offer opportunities for refreshments and potentially accommodation. Given its length, planning stops for food and water is advisable.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other official trails. You will find it covers parts of the [CIMA CA02] Piedrasluengas * Puentenansa (35%), [CIMA CA02] Piedrasluengas * Potes (27%), Transcantábrica (14%), and [CIMA CA16] Collado de Hoz * La Hermida (11%).

waypoints.name: Linares to La Hermida Mountain Road
